comments.body: Do you want to share one Random instance among different threads ? Or make sure each thread has its own instance of Random ?

According to the documentation for Random sharing one Random object between threads is a poor idea. I just want to control the seed of the random objects being used so the behavior is consistent and repeatable.
You could inject a seed with reflection (set initialized to false, call setSeed and set initialized back to true).
Usually, there's no such thing like consistency when working with multiple threads. Your solution is fine, just a bit slower than ThreadLocalRandom, especially in JDK8.

1. In Java 8, there are designated fields in Thread for ThreadLocalRandom, so ThreadLocal is not used any more. 2. While using ThreadLocal<Random> does eliminate contention, unnecessary checking is still on in Random. Therefore its performance would not be comparable to ThreadLocalRandom (even in Java 7).
@kavinyao uncontended synchronization in java is quite fast these days.

answers.body: The code for ThreadLocalRandom appears to be implemented as a ThreadLocal anyway (not exactly like you've put it, but probably close enough). I think what you have will work great.

You can simply use Random, just make sure each Random object is only accessed within a single thread.

Random, being an ancient class like Vector, is unnecessarily heavily synchronized. They probably wanted to show off Java's threading support since it was a big deal at that time. Also Java was mostly intended to run on consumer PCs which mostly had a single processor so synchronization didn't impact scaling like it does today on multiprocessors.

Now an obvious answer is to provide a thread-unsafe version of Random, just like providing the thread-unsfae ArrayList as the alternative to Vector. That didn't happen, instead, we got ThreadLocalRandom. That is kind of odd, not sure what's the motivation behind that. In java8, ThreadLocalRandom is further optimized to operate directly on some int fields in the Thread object.

First, your code uses the same seed for every thread. That can be a security problem. Moreover, every access to the membery of your instance of Random is synchronized, so it will be slower than the corresponding methods of ThreadLocalRandom (which are not synchronized). If the compiler could be sure, that your instance of Random does not escape the current Thread, it could optimize those "synchronized" away. but As you can store shared references in ThreadLocal there is no way for the compiler to check this.

Depends on nature of seed required and ThreadLocalRandom does not allow setting the Random seed. There are cases where you may want to seed Random by, say, "user" from current request so "randomness is consistent for a user" and as containers reuse threads (few) for new requests and new Random() is expensive, you may want to create your own ThreadLocal. As Java ThreadLocalRandom does not support seed, you can write it easily in JDK 1.8:

public class RandomUtil {

 private static final ThreadLocal<Random> RANDOM_THREAD_LOCAL = ThreadLocal.withInitial(Random::new);

 public static Random threadLocalRandom(long seed){
    Random random = RANDOM_THREAD_LOCAL.get();
    random.setSeed(seed);
    return random;
}

As each Random is local to a thread and thread safe, it can be re seeded "if needed" for next request/user without creating a new Random(). This should way performant than creating new Random(user seed) for each request but slower than Java ThreadLocalRandom with its internal optimizations.

tl;dr

Just use ThreadLocalRandom unless your situation demands security precautions or setting the seed.

OpenJDK

Most JDK/JRE products available are based largely, if not entirely, on the source code published by the OpenJDK project. That codebase is open-source. So we can study the source code to address your Question.

Innards

At least in Java 25, and likely earlier versions, there is only one ThreadLocalRandom object (source code). A single instance of ThreadLocalRandom is created eagerly upon class loading, a private static final singleton.

Every thread shares this same object, accessed via the static method ThreadLocalRandom.current().

However, the randomness state (the seed, secondary seed, and probe) specific to each thread is stored directly in three int fields of each Thread object (source code). Apparently these were accessed via Unsafe for efficiency in past versions, but not in Java 25. After the default zero, the values of those ints are initialized lazily on first use of ThreadLocalRandom.current() by that thread. See source code.

Security

One limitation of ThreadLocalRandom is security. As the Javadoc cautions:

Instances of ThreadLocalRandom are not cryptographically secure. Consider instead using SecureRandom in security-sensitive applications. Additionally, default-constructed instances do not use a cryptographically random seed unless the system property java.util.secureRandomSeed is set to true.

You can set that property programmatically. I imagine you must do this before loading the ThreadLocalRandom class.

System.setProperty("java.util.secureRandomSeed", "true");

But if your stakeholders have security concerns, I imagine you will want to exercise more control through use of SecureRandom than ThreadLocalRandom can offer.

Cannot set seed

Another limitation, for some use-cases, of ThreadLocalRandom is the not being able to set the seed. The Random#setSeed method inherited by ThreadLocalRandom throws an UnsupportedOperationException always.

Conclusion: Generally prefer ThreadLocalRandom in modern Java

👉🏽 So I see no reason not to use ThreadLocalRandom for general purposes without security concerns. This approach is efficient and practical.

  • Doing so is quite efficient, involving a singleton, and access to three int fields already existing on every thread.
  • Using a Random involves contention when accessed across threads simultaneously. Using ThreadLocalRandom avoids that contention.
  • ThreadLocalRandom is fully compatible and safe with platform (traditional) threads as well as with virtual threads.

Using ThreadLocalRandom is contraindicated if:

  • Your situation carries security concerns.
  • Your situation needs to set the seed.

Example code

Here is an example of using ThreadLocalRandom to simulate a multi-threaded workload. Each task sleeps for a random number of seconds generated by ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextInt.

// Simulate varying amount of work being performed in each thread
// by waiting for a random amount of time.
Instant start = Instant.now();
IO.println( "Demo start: " + start );
try (
        ExecutorService executorService = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() ;
)
{
    for ( int ordinal = 1 ; ordinal <= 10 ; ordinal++ )
    {
        final int finalOrdinal = ordinal;  // Need `final` for access from lambda below.
        executorService.execute( ( ) -> {
            int randomSeconds = ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextInt( 7 , 42 );
            Duration duration = Duration.ofSeconds( randomSeconds );
            IO.println( "Thread ID " + Thread.currentThread().threadId() + " busy working from " + Instant.now() + " to elapse: " + duration );
            try { Thread.sleep( duration ); } catch ( InterruptedException e ) { throw new RuntimeException( e ); }
            // Beware: Output to console does NOT necessarily appear in chronological order when accessed across threads. Use and study timestamps if you care.
            IO.println( "Thread ID " + Thread.currentThread().threadId() + " completed task # " + finalOrdinal + " at " + Instant.now() );
        } );
    }
}
Instant done = Instant.now();
IO.println( "Demo done at " + done + ". Elapsed: " + Duration.between( start , done ) );

When run:

Demo start: 2025-10-05T23:21:56.235942Z
Thread ID 27 busy working from 2025-10-05T23:21:56.243453Z to elapse: PT23S
Thread ID 45 busy working from 2025-10-05T23:21:56.243838Z to elapse: PT24S

title: Should I prefer ThreadLocalRandom over ThreadLocal<Random>?
body:
I'm looking to use (seeded) Random objects across multiple threads, and the javadocs pointed me to ThreadLocalRandom which looks great except I can't set the seed, so I can't ensure consistency among different threads or runs. Is there any practical reason to use ThreadLocalRandom or would it be acceptable to do something like the following:

// Pass returned ThreadLocal object to all threads which need it
public static ThreadLocal<Random> threadRandom(final long seed) {
    return new ThreadLocal<Random>(){
        @Override
        protected Random initialValue() {
            return new Random(seed);
        }
    };
}
